text: Hello friends, this is a case of inguinal hernia in a female patient. This patient has right sided inguinal hernia and she has direct inguinal hernia and that is recurrent inguinal hernia. She was already operated before but miss was not applied and it has recurred. So initially the diagnosis was bilateral inguinal hernia. But once we entered inside then we saw that it was only direct right sided. We can see right side there is one small scar also as you can see. And now we will go by laparoscopy. So this is the supraumblycal port and this is the varus middle which has been introduced.

The patient is positioned supine for the procedure. The surgeon uses an Ethicon Veress needle, noting its indicator that changes from red to green when the tip is correctly positioned. The needle is inserted at a 90-degree angle to the abdominal wall and a 45-degree angle to the patient's body, with the abdominal wall lifted. A syringe with saline is used for the irrigation test, ensuring free flow to confirm proper placement.

The surgeon performs the irrigation test followed by the suction test, observing bubbles indicating a successful suction test. The hanging drop test is conducted by lifting the abdominal wall, allowing fluid to enter, confirming a positive result. Tubing is attached to the insufflator to create pneumoperitoneum with a pressure of fifteen and a flow rate of one liter per minute. The actual pressure gradually increases, and liver dullness is obliterated, producing a tympanic sound similar to a ripe watermelon. The incision is enlarged, and the first trocar is introduced through the supraumbilical port.

The surgeon introduces the telescope, completing white balancing and focusing. Gas is visible and audible as it enters the abdominal cavity, with transillumination showing light through the skin at the hernia site. Two epsilon ports are placed according to the baseball diamond concept. The omentum is seen protruding into the hernia sac and is gently pulled out using an atraumatic grasper. The direct right-sided inguinal hernia is identified between the medial and lateral umbilical ligaments. The inferior epigastric vessels, medial umbilical ligament, and median umbilical ligament are visible. The left side shows no hernia, and the bladder and uterus appear normal, with the uterus slightly enlarged due to multiparity.

The surgeon identifies the right ovary and fallopian tube, then pushes small bubbles upward by tilting the patient's head 30 degrees down. The right ureter is visible with peristalsis. The sigmoid colon is gently pushed to the left, revealing the sacral promontory and median sacral vessels. The round ligament is seen finishing at the deep ring, and the triangle of doom is identified with the external iliac artery and vein. The anterior longitudinal ligament is visible, relevant for sacral colpopexy or rectopexy procedures.

The surgeon identifies the external cardiac vein, cecum, omentum, and tinea coli. The small intestine and the base of the appendix are visible, with the appendix positioned retrocecal. The triangle of pain is noted lateral to the triangle of doom, containing the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) and genitofemoral nerve (GFN). A direct right-sided inguinal hernia is observed between the inferior epigastric vessel and medial umbilical ligament. The bladder appears distended, and no hernia is present on the left side. The surgeon plans to place an ipsilateral port lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel and begins creating a pre-peritoneal pocket by stretching and cutting the peritoneum inferior medially.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum, allowing gas to enter, and grasps the lower edge of the punctured peritoneum. Using the tip of a harmonic scalpel or scissors, the surgeon carefully cuts the peritoneum up to the medial umbilical ligament while stretching it inferomedially to avoid injuring the inferior epigastric vessel. The medial umbilical ligament is visible. The surgeon then begins lateral dissection using blunt instruments, pushing the peritoneum inferomedially and separating the transversalis fascia. The lateral dissection continues until the spermatic vessels, marking the medial boundary of the triangle of pain and the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom, are identified.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the anterior peritoneum area, avoiding the use of energy to prevent nerve injury. The arc of the transversus abdominis muscle is visible as the peritoneum is separated. The medial umbilical ligament is stretched inferomedially to access the space of the radius. The surgeon identifies the Cooper's ligament, referred to as the 'lighthouse,' while avoiding manipulation of the hernia sac.

The shiny Cooper ligament becomes visible as the surgeon dissects the hernia sac, which initially covers the area. The sac is separated from the transversalis fascia, revealing the pearly white fascia. The surgeon carefully pulls the peritoneum away from the transversalis fascia, pushing the pseudosac back into the defect while extracting the sac and any associated lipoma. The Cooper ligament is now clearly visible below the tendinous arc of the levator ani muscle.

The obturator nerve is visible near the bladder in the space of Retzius. The Cooper ligament on the opposite side and the pubic symphysis bone are clearly visible. The surgeon performs lateral dissection to enlarge the pocket, ensuring the mesh will not crumple. The Cooper ligament, inferior epigastric vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster are all visible. The lateral transversus abdominis is also visible, and a simple proline mesh is prepared for use.

The surgeon folds a 15x12 cm mesh and inserts it into the cannula using a grasper. The mesh is then spread across the surgical site using two graspers, ensuring the inferomedial end covers the Cooper ligament. The mesh is positioned so its center aligns with the center of the defect, covering the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. Care is taken to ensure the mesh is more medial for indirect hernias and slightly lateral for direct hernias.

The surgeon fixes the mesh over the Cooper ligament and laterally above the inguinal ligament over the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle. The fixation is carefully placed to prevent mesh migration, avoiding over-fixation. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, and fixation is done lateral to it. The center of the mesh is aligned with the center of the defect. Suturing is planned using the Dundee jamming knot technique for continuous suturing, with the Aberdeen termination method.

The surgeon takes a bite on the anterior peritoneum and another on the posterior peritoneum over the medial umbilical ligament, passing the needle through the loop to apply a self-locking knot. The continuous suturing technique involves taking bites on both the upper and lower edges of the peritoneum, keeping the sutures slightly loose for later adjustment.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing using an endoske needle, pressing it against the bowel surface to facilitate the suturing process. The peritoneal sac, initially visible in the defect, is left intact as the peritoneum's natural ability to shrink and regenerate is relied upon. The suturing is done with a loose tension, ensuring flexibility in the peritoneal tissue.

The surgeon takes a bite on the lateral edge of the peritoneum and then another deeper bite in an undissected area, pulling the suture laterally towards the upper abdomen. The peritoneum closes automatically as the suture is pulled parallel to the abdominal wall. The insufflation pressure is reduced to prevent cutting through the tissue. Aberdeen termination is performed by creating a loop within a loop, pulling the needle through, and locking the suture, ensuring complete peritoneal closure with no mesh visible.

The surgeon completes the closure of the peritoneum with sutures, using scissors to cut the suture and remove it. The entire peritoneum is visibly closed, and the needle is extracted, concluding the transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair in a female patient.
